narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at your academic record, test scores, family background, and other details to forecast your bar exam outcome. For you specifically, it estimated a 60% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt—a threshold that led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile. This low performance signal stood out strongly to the model, as it often correlates with challenges on high-stakes exams like the bar. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 36, which, while solid, fell slightly short of the dataset average of about 36.7 and pulled the prediction in a positive direction by suggesting decent reasoning skills, but not enough to offset other concerns. Rounding out the top factors was your Year 1 decile of 2, again indicating early underperformance relative to peers averaging around the 6th decile, which nudged the model further toward expecting bar exam struggles. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.8 (below the 3.21 average), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status played supporting roles but weren't the primary drivers here. Compared to typical admitted students, who often show stronger consistent decile rankings and slightly higher test scores, your profile highlighted early and mid-academic hurdles that the model weighed heavily. This doesn't define your potential—many students overcome similar starts through targeted improvement. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic deciles with additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong recommendation letters that highlight your resilience as a Black man navigating these challenges. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the model's view in your favor.
